name: Foundations & governance
description: The vocabulary and scaffolding every risk function shares.
primer:
**Risk** is the potential for financial loss from an adverse event — quantifiable (known probabilities), unlike Knightian *uncertainty*. A firm earns its return by *holding* risks it can manage, not eliminating them.

**Appetite vs capacity.** *Capacity* is the maximum risk the balance sheet can absorb before threatening solvency. **Risk appetite** — the risk a firm chooses to take, set by the **board** in a Risk Appetite Statement — is always strictly *inside* capacity. **Risk tolerance** is the acceptable band around a specific limit. **Inherent risk** is exposure before controls; **residual risk** is what remains after hedging, collateral, insurance and controls — and *capital is held against residual, not inherent, risk*.

**Risk–return:** higher expected return demands higher risk; investors require a *risk premium* for undiversifiable risk, so $\\text{required return}=r_f+\\text{premium}$.

**Loss split.** *Expected loss* $\\mathrm{EL}=\\mathrm{PD}\\times\\mathrm{LGD}\\times\\mathrm{EAD}$ is covered by **provisions/pricing** (a cost of doing business). *Unexpected loss* — volatility of loss around the mean, $\\mathrm{UL}\\approx\\mathrm{VaR}_\\alpha(L)-\\mathrm{EL}$ — is absorbed by **capital**.

**Mitigants.** *Hedging* takes an offsetting position (basis risk remains). *Netting* offsets mutual obligations to a single net figure (close-out netting is the bedrock of counterparty risk). *Collateral/margin*: initial margin covers potential future exposure, variation margin settles daily MtM. *Notional* is the reference amount, **not** the amount at risk. *Mark-to-market* values at current price (mark-to-model adds model risk).

**Diversification** lowers *unexpected* loss (volatility) by combining exposures with $\\rho_{ij}<1$ — $\\sigma_p^2=\\sum_i w_i^2\\sigma_i^2+\\sum_{i\\ne j}w_iw_j\\rho_{ij}\\sigma_i\\sigma_j$ — but **never reduces expected loss**; it fails when correlations rise toward 1 in a crisis. **Concentration** is the opposite failure: the **Herfindahl** index $\\mathrm{HHI}=\\sum_i w_i^2$ rises toward 1 as a portfolio concentrates.

**Governance.** *Three lines of defence*: (1) the business owns its risks; (2) independent risk & compliance challenge; (3) **internal audit** assures the whole framework. Tools: a *risk register* (inventory of risks), *key risk indicators* (KRIs, early-warning metrics), *P&L attribution* (decompose P&L into factors; large residuals flag a missing factor). *RAROC* $=\\dfrac{\\text{return}-\\mathrm{EL}}{\\text{economic capital}}$ charges each activity for the capital its risk consumes; it creates value when above the cost of capital. *Economic capital* is the firm's own estimate (typically the **99.9%** loss quantile, matching a target rating); *regulatory capital* is the external minimum.

**Systemic notions.** *Tail risk*: rare, severe outcomes; fat tails make them likelier than a normal model implies. The *black swan* (popularised by **Taleb**) is a high-impact event rationalised only after the fact. *Procyclicality*: risk measures amplify the cycle (low vol begets leverage); countercyclical buffers dampen it. *Moral hazard*: a party insulated from a risk takes on more of it (e.g. bailout expectations).

cards.definition: The uncertainty of future outcomes — concretely, the potential for financial loss arising from an adverse event. Risk is measured, priced and capitalised rather than simply eliminated; a firm earns its return precisely by holding risks it can manage.
cards.note: Distinguish risk (quantifiable probabilities) from Knightian uncertainty (probabilities unknown).

cards.term: Risk appetite — the aggregate amount and type of risk a firm is willing to take — is ultimately set by the {{board}}.
cards.definition: It is expressed in a Risk Appetite Statement and cascaded into concrete limits per desk, portfolio and risk type.
cards.note: Appetite is a choice; capacity is the ceiling the balance sheet can bear.

cards.definition: The acceptable variation around a specific target or limit — the band within which a metric may move before action is required. Tolerances operationalise the broader appetite at the level of individual exposures.

cards.term: Risk appetite is always set strictly inside risk capacity.
cards.definition: True. Capacity is the maximum risk a firm can absorb given its capital, liquidity and regulatory constraints before threatening solvency; appetite, the risk the firm chooses to take, is always set strictly inside that ceiling.
cards.summary: True — appetite is a chosen amount of risk set strictly below capacity, the maximum the firm can absorb.

cards.term: Capital is held against inherent risk, the exposure before any controls are applied.
cards.definition: False. Inherent risk is the exposure before controls; residual risk is what remains after hedging, collateral, insurance and controls. Capital is held against residual, not inherent, risk.
cards.summary: False — capital is held against residual risk, after controls and mitigants, not inherent risk.

cards.definition: The principle that higher expected return can only be obtained by accepting higher risk; rational investors demand compensation (a risk premium) for bearing undiversifiable risk.
The average loss anticipated on an exposure over a horizon — the amount a prudent firm provisions for in advance. In credit it factors into default probability, severity and exposure.
cards.note: Covered by provisions/pricing; it is a cost of doing business, not a surprise.

cards.definition: The volatility of loss around its expected value — the deviation that capital, not provisions, must absorb. It is typically measured as a high quantile of the loss distribution minus the mean.

Taking an offsetting position whose value moves opposite to an existing exposure, reducing net sensitivity to a chosen risk factor. A perfect hedge removes the risk entirely; in practice basis risk remains.

cards.term: Diversification reduces a portfolio's expected loss.
cards.definition: False. Combining less-than-perfectly-correlated exposures lets independent losses partly cancel, lowering portfolio volatility (unexpected loss) for a given expected return — it never reduces expected loss itself.
cards.note: Works only because $\rho_{ij}<1$; correlations rising toward 1 in a crisis is why diversification fails when most needed.
cards.summary: False — diversification lowers unexpected loss (volatility), but never reduces expected loss.

cards.definition: Offsetting mutual obligations between two parties so only the net amount is owed, shrinking gross exposure to a single figure. Close-out netting on default is the legal bedrock of derivative counterparty risk management.
cards.note: Enforceable netting can cut counterparty exposure by an order of magnitude.

cards.definition: Assets pledged to secure an exposure, seized if the counterparty defaults. Initial margin covers potential future exposure; variation margin settles daily mark-to-market moves so exposure is reset toward zero.
The reference amount on which a contract's payments are computed — not the amount at risk. A large notional can carry small market risk; exposure depends on sensitivities, not face value.
cards.formula: a €10M interest-rate swap exchanges payments on 10M; its risk is a few thousand €/bp

cards.definition: Valuing a position at its current market price rather than book or historical cost. MtM drives daily P&L, collateral calls and risk metrics; for illiquid positions it shades into mark-to-model.
cards.note: Mark-to-model introduces model risk where observable prices are absent.

cards.definition: Decomposing realised profit and loss into the risk factors that produced it (rates, spreads, FX, vol, carry, residual). Large unexplained residuals are a red flag that the risk model is missing a factor.
cards.note: A core control linking the front-office model to actual outcomes.

cards.term: In the three-lines-of-defence model, the third line that objectively assures the whole framework is {{internal audit}}.
cards.definition: The standard risk-governance model: (1) the business owns and manages its risks day-to-day; (2) independent risk management and compliance set policy and challenge; (3) the third line assures the whole framework objectively.
cards.note: Independence of the second and third lines from revenue is the point.

cards.definition: A maintained inventory of identified risks with their assessed likelihood, impact, owner, controls and mitigation status. It is the operational backbone of enterprise risk management.

A measurable metric that signals changing risk levels and serves as an early warning ahead of losses — e.g. limit-breach frequency, trade-error rate, system downtime, or staff turnover on a critical desk.

A profitability measure that charges each activity for the economic capital its risk consumes, enabling comparison across businesses with different risk profiles. Activities are value-creating when RAROC exceeds the cost of capital.

cards.term: Economic capital is typically calibrated to a high loss quantile of {{99.9%}}, matching a target credit rating.
cards.definition: A firm's own estimate of the capital needed to remain solvent over a horizon at a chosen confidence level, given its actual risk profile.
cards.note: Contrast with regulatory capital, the externally imposed minimum.

cards.definition: The risk of rare, severe outcomes in the extreme tail of the loss distribution — events far from the mean that dominate solvency concerns. Fat-tailed distributions make these far likelier than a normal model implies.

cards.term: The concept of a high-impact, unforeseen event rationalised only after the fact was popularised by {{Taleb}}.
cards.definition: A high-impact event that lies outside ordinary expectations and is rationalised only after the fact. The risk lesson is humility: models calibrated on benign history systematically understate the unprecedented.

The tendency of risk measures and rules to amplify the cycle — risk looks low and leverage builds in booms, then measures spike and force deleveraging in busts. Countercyclical buffers exist to dampen this feedback.
cards.note: VaR and margin models are inherently procyclical: low vol begets high leverage.

cards.definition: The distortion in which a party insulated from a risk's consequences takes on more of it — e.g. a bank expecting a bailout. Much prudential regulation exists to counter the moral hazard of implicit guarantees.

cards.term: A common measure of portfolio concentration that rises toward 1 as exposure concentrates is the {{Herfindahl}} index.
cards.definition: Loss potential from outsized exposure to a single name, sector, region or risk factor, so that one event causes disproportionate damage. It is the failure mode diversification is meant to prevent and is penalised in large-exposure rules.
cards.formula: a Herfindahl index $\mathrm{HHI}=\sum_i w_i^2$ rises toward 1 as a portfolio concentrates
